title: Quick check: When to break the rules

questions.prompt: Three checks before deviating from a default?

choices.text: (1) Have you tried the default first cleanly — not a half-hearted version? Many 'this doesn't work for me' claims come from never running the standard pattern long enough to read. (2) Do you have data showing the default fails — hypothesis isn't data; run the test. (3) Can you articulate WHY the default fails for your case — not just restating that it does. If all three check out, deviating is right; otherwise the default is your safest bet.

questions.explanation: Tried default, have data, can articulate why. All three required.

questions.prompt: B2B SaaS account selling to 200k decision-makers at specific company types. Broad targeting and ASC failing. What's the justified deviation?

choices.text: Switch to narrow targeting (CRM upload of target accounts, or title + industry targeting), manual campaigns instead of ASC, and likely cost cap to control CPA. The pool is structurally too small (200k) for broad to be efficient; broad would waste 99% of impressions on irrelevant users. ASC's broad-by-design fights the offer's actual audience. The narrow + manual + cost cap combination is the documented exception case: small structural audience, specific filter requirements, predictable CPA target. Run that pattern for 4 weeks before judging.

questions.explanation: B2B with narrow structural audience justifies all three deviations together.
